chapter: Nelson Mandela: Long Walk to Freedom
subject: English

flashcards.front: What is 'Apartheid'?
flashcards.back: 'Apartheid' was a political system in South Africa that rigorously separated people according to their race, enforcing racial segregation and discrimination, particularly against non-white populations.

flashcards.front: Who was Nelson Mandela and what was his primary struggle?
flashcards.back: Nelson Mandela was a prominent anti-apartheid revolutionary who spent a lifetime fighting against the system of apartheid in South Africa. He became the first black President of South Africa.

flashcards.front: What was the African National Congress (ANC)?
flashcards.back: The African National Congress (ANC) was Nelson Mandela's political party. It spearheaded the fight against apartheid and later won South Africa's first democratic elections in 1994, forming the first non-racial government.

flashcards.front: How long did Nelson Mandela spend in prison for his activism?
flashcards.back: Nelson Mandela spent thirty years in prison for his activism and struggle against the apartheid regime in South Africa.

flashcards.front: When did the democratic elections take place in South Africa, leading to Mandela becoming President?
flashcards.back: Democratic elections were held in South Africa in 1994. Nelson Mandela was inaugurated as the first black President on 10 May 1994, marking the end of more than three centuries of white rule.

flashcards.front: What historic occasion is Mandela referring to in this extract from his autobiography?
flashcards.back: Mandela is referring to his inauguration ceremony as the first black President of South Africa, which marked the installation of the country's first democratic, non-racial government.

flashcards.front: Where did the inauguration ceremony take place?
flashcards.back: The inauguration ceremony took place in the lovely sandstone amphitheatre formed by the Union Buildings in Pretoria, South Africa.

flashcards.front: What was the historical significance of the Union Buildings amphitheatre before the inauguration?
flashcards.back: For decades, the Union Buildings amphitheatre in Pretoria had been 'the seat of white supremacy,' symbolizing the center of racial superiority and apartheid rule.

flashcards.front: What did Mandela mean by a 'rainbow gathering' at the inauguration?
flashcards.back: A 'rainbow gathering' referred to the beautiful coming together of various peoples – blacks, whites, and coloureds – along with dignitaries from over 140 countries, celebrating a new non-racial and democratic South Africa.

flashcards.front: How many seats did Nelson Mandela's ANC party win in the first democratic elections?
flashcards.back: Nelson Mandela's African National Congress (ANC) party won 252 of the 400 seats in the first democratic elections of South Africa's history.

flashcards.front: What solemn promise did Nelson Mandela make in his inaugural address regarding the future of South Africa?
flashcards.back: Nelson Mandela solemnly pledged, 'Never, never and never again shall it be that this beautiful land will again experience the oppression of one by another.' This promised an end to all forms of oppression.

flashcards.front: What does 'political emancipation' mean in the context of Mandela's speech?
flashcards.back: 'Political emancipation' refers to freedom from restriction or bondage. In this context, it signifies South Africa's achievement of freedom from racial oppression, discrimination, and the various forms of suffering caused by apartheid.

flashcards.front: Besides ending racial oppression, what other forms of liberation did Mandela pledge for his people?
flashcards.back: Mandela pledged to liberate all his people from the continuing bondage of poverty, deprivation, suffering, gender discrimination, and other forms of discrimination.

flashcards.front: Why did Mandela refer to his people as 'outlaws not so long ago'?
flashcards.back: South Africa, due to its apartheid policy, was previously isolated by many countries, which had broken off diplomatic relations. This made its leaders and people 'outlaws' in the international community.

flashcards.front: What 'extraordinary human disaster' is Mandela referring to in his speech?
flashcards.back: Mandela is referring to the brutal and inhumane system of apartheid, which caused immense suffering, discrimination, and division among the people of South Africa for many decades.

flashcards.front: Describe the general atmosphere and significance of May 10th, 1994, as described in the chapter.
flashcards.back: May 10th dawned bright and clear, symbolizing a new beginning. It was a day of joy, celebration, and hope, marked by an unprecedented gathering of international leaders, signifying the end of white rule and the birth of a democratic, non-racial South Africa.

flashcards.front: Who accompanied Nelson Mandela on the podium during the inauguration ceremony?
flashcards.back: Nelson Mandela was accompanied by his daughter, Zenani, on the podium during the inauguration ceremony.

flashcards.front: Who were sworn in as the First and Second Deputy Presidents during the ceremony?
flashcards.back: Mr. de Klerk was first sworn in as the second deputy president, and Thabo Mbeki was then sworn in as the first deputy president.

flashcards.front: Define 'deprivation' as used in Mandela's speech.
flashcards.back: 'Deprivation' refers to the state of not having one's rightful benefits or basic necessities, which was a widespread consequence of apartheid for the black population in South Africa.

flashcards.front: How did the international community respond to the inauguration of Mandela as President?
flashcards.back: The inauguration was attended by politicians and dignitaries from more than 140 countries around the world, showcasing strong international support for the new democratic South Africa and its victory over apartheid.
